title: Unstable Radix Sort with Incorrect Edge Handling

statement.markdown: You're given a large integer array that contains only numbers between 1 and 10^6. Your goal is to sort the array using the Radix Sort algorithm. Radix sort is a non-comparative integer sorting algorithm that sorts data with integer keys by grouping keys by the individual digits which share the same significant position and value.
statement.inputFormat: A single line of integers, separated by spaces, with 1 <= n <= 10000.
statement.outputFormat: Sorted numbers in the same format.
statement.notes: Use the least significant digit first, and sort in place.

examples.explanation: Example input with a small number of elements. Only the least significant digit (ones place) is used for sorting.

hints.text: Radix sort is a non-comparative algorithm that works based on the digits of the numbers.
The least significant digit of the numbers is used for sorting.

editorial.title: Editorial for Unstable Radix Sort with Incorrect Edge Handling
editorial.intuition: In this problem, you need to fix the implementation of the Radix Sort algorithm to handle edge cases correctly.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach would be using a sorting algorithm like Bubble Sort or Merge Sort and then implementing radix sort on top of it.
editorial.betterSolution: Radix sort can be implemented in-place directly by storing elements in buckets and then reconstructing the array
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution for this problem is to use the counting sort algorithm as the base sort and then use radix sort to count occurrences of digits in each bucket.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correct approach uses digit counting to sort numbers. It handles edge cases correctly and ensures correct results.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run of the correct approach.
